review_text: I went here whilst staying at The Venetian hotel over New Year's Eve and honestly had a terrible experience. I was very excited to visit the bakery as I used to watch cake boss on TV and absolutely LOVED the show. I tried the lobster tail which is highly overrated, the cupcakes which were dry and a few other pastries that aren't even worth mentioning as I don't even remember anymore they left no impression on me. Definitely does not deserve to have its own tv show- there are much better bakeries out there.

fixes: Deploy a standardized sensory evaluation sheet (e.g., ISO 8589‑based) for every lobster batch, requiring chefs to record texture, flavor intensity, and overall satisfaction scores before service; any batch scoring below 8/10 triggers immediate recipe adjustment. | - Install IoT temperature sensors on all seafood storage units (e.g., SmarterChef Smart Temp) and integrate alerts into the kitchen’s Slack channel so staff are notified in real time if temperatures deviate from the optimal 0 °C range, preventing over‑cooking. | - Implement a weekly supplier quality audit checklist in the restaurant’s ERP (e.g., Toast POS) that records freshness date, origin certification, and visual inspection results; suppliers falling short of the predefined criteria receive a corrective‑action ticket in Asana within 24 hours. | - Create a “Hype vs. Reality” menu card prototype with QR‑linked Google Forms feedback, asking diners to rate the lobster against expectations; analyze responses daily and adjust portion size, garnish, or pricing accordingly when average ratings fall below 4 stars.

review_text: I was so excited to go here, but that excitement soon turned to disappointment. We had never been to a Carlo's Bakery in person, and we are fans of Buddy, of course, so we had to go while in Vegas. We waited in a decently long line, which gave us time to look at all the things that were wrong with this place. The display cases were filthy and messy. Frosting, sprinkles and crumbs were everywhere. There were even some cupcakes that had fallen upside down that just laid there in the case looking oh so sad. Several of the trays were empty, one of those being cannolis. One of the main reasons we wanted to go to Carlo's was was to try one of their famous cannolis. Sadly, that didn't happen. Mind you this was in the middle of the day. It wasn't like we were there 10 minutes til close. On top of that the food that was in the case was not properly labeled. Many things had no labels at all. This can be dangerous for folks with food allergies and annoying to other people trying to communicate what they want to the workers. We ended up getting a lobster tail and a napoleon type thing (a layered cake. no sign, so who knows what it was!). The lobster tails tasted stale and like it had sat in a fridge for a week. The cake thing was just not good. The flavor was not good. I took one bite of each and wished I had spent that $20 on a slot machine instead of desserts that wal-mart can do better than. I really hope Buddy and his family get a handle on things and get their locations back in shape, because I don't think this is the quality they had in mind for their customers.
issue: Display cases were filthy and messy with frosting, sprinkles, crumbs, and fallen cupcakes
fixes: Deploy a daily “Case‑Clean” checklist in iAuditor (SafetyCulture) that assigns a staff member to wipe down each display case with approved cleaning solution, log completion time, and trigger automatic escalation if any item is missed twice in a row. | - Install QR‑code stickers on each case linking to a one‑click Workforce Management task in Deputy; when scanned, the system creates a 15‑minute window for cleaning, automatically notifying the assigned shift lead via Slack. | - Schedule a weekly deep‑clean rotation using a standardized SOP documented in Confluence, with a KPI of ≤ 2 minutes per case and a real‑time compliance dashboard in Power BI that flags any deviation from the target. | - Integrate RFID sensors (e.g., Zebra MotionWorks) into case lids to detect when they are opened, generating an instant alert to the floor supervisor’s mobile device so cleaning can be performed before the next product placement.

review_text: I was so excited to go here, but that excitement soon turned to disappointment. We had never been to a Carlo's Bakery in person, and we are fans of Buddy, of course, so we had to go while in Vegas. We waited in a decently long line, which gave us time to look at all the things that were wrong with this place. The display cases were filthy and messy. Frosting, sprinkles and crumbs were everywhere. There were even some cupcakes that had fallen upside down that just laid there in the case looking oh so sad. Several of the trays were empty, one of those being cannolis. One of the main reasons we wanted to go to Carlo's was was to try one of their famous cannolis. Sadly, that didn't happen. Mind you this was in the middle of the day. It wasn't like we were there 10 minutes til close. On top of that the food that was in the case was not properly labeled. Many things had no labels at all. This can be dangerous for folks with food allergies and annoying to other people trying to communicate what they want to the workers. We ended up getting a lobster tail and a napoleon type thing (a layered cake. no sign, so who knows what it was!). The lobster tails tasted stale and like it had sat in a fridge for a week. The cake thing was just not good. The flavor was not good. I took one bite of each and wished I had spent that $20 on a slot machine instead of desserts that wal-mart can do better than. I really hope Buddy and his family get a handle on things and get their locations back in shape, because I don't think this is the quality they had in mind for their customers.
issue: The lobster tail tasted stale and the cake was poorly flavored, indicating low food quality
fixes: Deploy a daily “freshness audit” checklist in iAuditor (SafetyCulture) that records temperature logs, visual inspection scores, and expiration dates for all seafood; any item scoring below 85 % automatically generates a replacement ticket in the kitchen’s Trello board. | - Install IoT‑enabled humidity/temperature sensors (e.g., Sensaphone) on seafood storage units and configure alerts to the kitchen manager via Slack when conditions deviate from the optimal range (0‑4 °C, ≤60 % RH). | - Introduce a standardized sensory evaluation protocol using a calibrated flavor wheel; chefs record taste, aroma, and texture scores for every baked good batch in a Google Sheet, triggering a corrective recipe adjustment if the average score falls below 80 %. | - Require suppliers to provide lot‑level traceability QR codes scanned at receipt; integrate the data into SAP MM so that any product older than 48 hours is flagged and automatically excluded from service.

review_text: Very disappointing, got the fabled Cannoli's and what an absolute waste of money. I had actually gotten some from a different bakery at Paris and assumed that as well as those tasted, Carlos's would be better, boy was I wrong! Buddy needs to stick to making cakes that look good, and I'm sure don't taste good.

fixes: Deploy a digital sensory‑evaluation checklist in Qualtrics (or a similar survey platform) for every batch; require ≥ 90 % score on “freshness” and “flavor” before products are released. | - Install real‑time temperature monitoring sensors linked to a dashboard (e.g., Monnit) on all pastry ovens and refrigerators, with automatic alerts when conditions deviate by more than ±2 °F from the target range. | - Conduct weekly supplier quality audits using the FoodLogiQ traceability module to verify ingredient lot numbers and freshness dates, flagging any batch older than 48 hours for rejection. | - Create a standardized recipe card in ChefTec that includes exact measurements, mixing times, and bake parameters, and mandate that chefs run a test batch at the start of each shift and log results in a Google Sheet KPI tracker.
rationale: The response directly addresses the issue of food quality and provides a detailed plan with actionable recommendations. It effectively employs concrete tools like Qualtrics, Monnit, FoodLogiQ, ChefTec, and Google Sheets, making it relevant, feasible, and measurable. The response also outlines specific processes such as the sensory-evaluation checklist and weekly supplier quality audits that can be directly implemented to improve food quality, thereby meeting the task requirements of providing 3-5 recommendations, each with a clear connection to a concrete tool or process.

review_text: Awful experience. Guest specialist Alyssa had a horrible attitude. She should be fired. The cashier Akberet, however, was very polite. Thumbs down for bad service!
issue: Guest specialist displayed a horrible attitude, leading to a bad service experience
fixes: Deploy a real‑time sentiment monitoring dashboard (e.g., Medallia Experience Cloud) that flags any “negative” interaction score within 15 minutes; automatically route those cases to a supervisor for immediate coaching. | - Require all guest specialists to complete a quarterly 360° feedback cycle in SurveyMonkey, with a minimum average rating of 4.5/5 on professionalism; tie results to performance bonuses and trigger corrective action if scores fall below target. | - Implement weekly 20‑minute role‑play drills on the Lessonly LMS where staff rehearse handling difficult customers, recording each session and reviewing it against a standardized scoring rubric. | - Create a “Zero‑Tolerance Escalation SOP” that mandates a documented escalation workflow in Zendesk: any guest complaint about rude behavior auto‑assigns to a senior manager and triggers a mandatory follow‑up call within 24 hours. | - Launch a peer‑shadowing program through the Workforce Management module in Kronos, pairing each specialist with a top‑rated colleague for one day per month to observe best‑practice interactions and capture learnings in a shared Google Sheet.

review_text: Place was horrible. First and foremost, we grabbed a number which was B89 and waited over 2 hours for our number to get called. One of the most disorganized companies I have ever seen. I understand things were busy but the way of doing business was horrible and we noticed a lot of people leaving purely because of the wait. Second, once we finally got our number called, I was informed the pie that I had ordered 4 hours earlier was not made. The manager came out and spoke to me and before even apologizing, blamed it on me because I had not pre-paid. She tried to explain how the process worked if I would have pre-paid for the pie but did not apologize or offer a solution outside of waiting another 40 minutes for my pie to be made. Absolute worst customer service I have ever seen from a "manager." On top of that, pre-paying for my pie was never even provided to me as an option which only made things worse. Would not recommend this to anyone unless you are looking to waste a few hours on the strip and if you don't want to get your order correct.
issue: Excessive wait time of over two hours before being called
fixes: Deploy a real‑time queue dashboard in Zendesk Explore that triggers an automatic alert to supervisors when average wait time exceeds 45 minutes, prompting immediate staffing adjustments. | - Integrate Twilio Flex with the call center’s CRM to route callers to the next available agent within 90 seconds and display live SLA compliance metrics on a wallboard. | - Implement predictive staffing using NICE Workforce Management to forecast peak call volume 15 minutes ahead and schedule additional agents during identified “high‑wait” windows. | - Launch an AI‑driven IVR chatbot (e.g., Ada) that resolves common inquiries and offers callback options, reducing inbound calls by at least 20 % and cutting average hold time.

review_text: Don't send your child in alone. The girl behind the counter told our grandson the machine was broken so his kid cup was filled only one-third of the way (about 5 tablespoons) and watery, although he still paid full price and tipped. When he returned to the car he was obviously disappointed so I went in to ask about it. The shift manager told me that's how they come now and then added, "Have a nice day." I guess rudeness and minion size portions is how they roll! As for us, we walked over to Culliver's where my grandson got a real size cup of ice cream with great customer service!
issue: Customer received a significantly smaller, watery portion of ice cream but was charged full price.
fixes: Deploy calibrated digital scales (e.g., Ohaus Scout) at the point‑of‑sale so every ice‑cream scoop is automatically weighed; set a threshold alert in the POS system when the weight falls below 70 % of the standard portion size. | - Install RFID‑enabled portion cups linked to the inventory management software (e.g., Oracle NetSuite) that logs exact cup fill volume per transaction and triggers a real‑time “underfill” flag if the volume is <90 %. | - Add a QR‑code receipt addendum generated by the POS that shows the actual gram count versus the advertised serving, enabling customers to verify immediately and submit a claim through the brand’s mobile app. | - Implement a weekly audit checklist in iAuditor where store managers record scoop weights and compare them against the target metric; any deviation >10 % generates an automatic ticket in ServiceNow for corrective action. | - Launch a rapid‑response compensation workflow in Salesforce Service Cloud that auto‑creates a coupon code for the next purchase within 24 hours whenever an underfilled order is flagged by the monitoring systems.
rationale: The response provides a clear action plan to address the customer issue of receiving smaller portions of ice cream than expected. It successfully references concrete tools and processes such as digital scales, RFID-enabled cups, QR-code receipts, iAuditor for audits, and Salesforce Service Cloud for compensation workflow. However, while these suggestions are actionable, they lack the explicit mention of cost and budget implications, which is a critical aspect in the rubric's feasibility criteria. The response also does not address how exactly these tools would be implemented or integrated with existing systems, which is an important factor in assessing their practicality. These omissions reduce the effectiveness of the recommendations and slightly decrease the score. Nevertheless, the response is still highly effective in providing a comprehensive plan to resolve the issue. So the overall score is 98.2.
